rationale: The citing paper uses Zhu and Meyers-Levy (2005) to support a core framing claim that background music can convey meaning and influence product-related judgments, not merely mood. It is cited in theory development when arguing that sensory ad elements can transfer nondiagnostic information to product perceptions, and this logic motivates the music-based tests in Study 2. This is more than a passing bibliography mention because the citation anchors a specific premise used to justify the paper's conceptual setup, even though the cited paper's embodied-versus-referential mechanism is not directly tested here.
relation_description: The 2017 paper treats Zhu and Meyers-Levy (2005) as prior foundational evidence that ad music affects product perceptions. It then builds from that premise to examine a different mechanism, showing that acoustic pitch in voice or music can shift perceived product size via cross-modal imagery.
